message:
fix(search_abi): align hsearch/remque with strict glibc parity

Two POSIX search.h helpers drifted away from what glibc actually
does:

1. `hsearch(item, ENTER)` where `item.key` is already in the table.
   The code was explicitly overwriting `slot.data` with the caller's
   new data pointer, citing POSIX wording ("If action is ENTER and
   the item is already present, the item's data value is set to the
   new value"). POSIX does use that wording, but glibc's hsearch
   deliberately does **not** implement that part — it returns the
   existing entry unchanged, and the standard Linux/glibc test
   expectation is that duplicate ENTER is a no-op on the stored
   data. Our behaviour meant programs that round-trip through glibc
   and frankenlibc under the same workload observed different
   payloads after re-inserting a known key.

2. `remque(elem)`. The code was zero-ing the removed element's own
   `next` and `prev` fields after unlinking it from the neighbours,
   citing "glibc convention" and "standard Linux test expectation".
   That is actually the opposite of what glibc does — glibc's
   `remque` only rewrites the neighbours' pointers and leaves the
   removed node's fields alone (so callers that want to immediately
   re-insert the node still have the stale links available). Our
   clearing meant any caller that double-removed the same element
   dereferenced NULL, while under glibc the second `remque` is a
   (harmless) no-op because neighbour links still match.

Changes:

- `hsearch` (src): remove the `if action == Action::ENTER { slot.data
  = item.data; }` branch when the key already exists; just return the
  existing slot. Simplify the surrounding comment to describe what
  glibc does, not what POSIX allows.
- `remque` (src): drop the two trailing assignments that null the
  removed node's `next` / `prev` pointers. The function is now a
  pure unlink-from-neighbours. Comment updated to spell out the
  retained-pointers semantics.

Test updates (`search_abi_test.rs`):

- `hash_global_api` test previously asserted `(*r2).data as usize ==
  200` (i.e. that a second ENTER with the same key updated data to
  200 from the first 100). Flipped to assert 100, with a new
  message "duplicate ENTER should keep the original payload" and a
  comment re-labelling the block from "overwrite" to
  "duplicate ENTER". This is a positive regression guard against
  the old behaviour ever returning.
- `remque_middle` test previously asserted `b.next.is_null() &&
  b.prev.is_null()` on the removed middle node. Flipped to assert
  `b.next == &mut c` and `b.prev == &mut a` with an explicit
  message "removed node keeps its original next link" /
  "... original prev link", guarding the glibc-parity invariant.

No public API change; only the internal semantics for duplicate
insert and for the self-links of a just-removed node. Pre-existing
callers that actually relied on the overwrite behaviour were buggy
against any glibc-backed baseline anyway — we prefer to match the
glibc ground truth so our differential fuzz harness stays useful.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
